critic_raw:
The treatment efficacy auxiliary label is a composite derived from clinical assessments within 14 days post-discharge. If hospital follow-up intensity (frequency of 14-day assessments) is correlated with readmission risk — which it is, as higher-risk patients are typically seen sooner — then the efficacy label availability itself encodes readmission risk. Patients without a 14-day assessment (missing efficacy labels) may be systematically lower risk. The design retains these patients with sensitivity analysis, but retaining them with missing auxiliary labels means the multi-task model receives a corrupted training signal for efficacy, while the single-task baseline is unaffected. This asymmetry could either harm or help the multi-task model.

Preprocessing is fit on the combined train+validation set before applying to test — this is not full-dataset leakage but it still means test-period distribution statistics are excluded. Importantly, the design states 'all transformers are fit on the combined train and validation set' — using validation data in preprocessing is non-standard and means the validation set cannot serve as a truly independent hyperparameter selection signal if its statistics have already influenced feature scaling.

Section 7 explicitly describes using the test set to verify model selection before final training ('a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set...this step is crucial to confirm that hyperparameter tuning was effective...After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data'). This is unambiguous test set contamination — the test set is used to make training decisions.

The 'efficiency' claim combines AUROC (higher is better) with parameter count and training time (lower is better). No statistical framework for comparing these dimensions is provided. Two models that differ slightly in AUROC but substantially in parameter count are not straightforwardly comparable — the claim of 'more efficient' requires a formal Pareto analysis or a defined tradeoff curve, neither of which is specified.

The efficacy auxiliary label measures post-discharge outcomes (medication adherence, functional status) that may be partially determined by the index hospitalization itself. If discharge quality correlates with readmission risk and also predicts efficacy, the auxiliary task may simply learn to predict readmission through the efficacy pathway — in which case multi-task learning is not introducing new signal but rather providing a proxy path to the same outcome.
all_issues_raised: The treatment efficacy label is derived from 14-day post-discharge assessments whose availability is correlated with readmission risk — missing efficacy labels are not random, and retaining these patients creates a corrupted auxiliary training signal that asymmetrically affects the multi-task model.
Section 7 explicitly states the test set is used to verify model selection and guide the decision to retrain on train+validation — this is direct test set contamination for training decisions.

Preprocessing transformers are fit on the combined train+validation set — using validation statistics in preprocessing means the validation set cannot serve as a truly independent model selection criterion.

The 'efficiency' hypothesis requires combining AUROC and model complexity into a single comparable quantity, but no formal Pareto analysis or tradeoff weighting function is specified — the claim is not operationally testable as stated.

The efficacy auxiliary outcome may be a proxy for readmission risk rather than an independent signal (discharge quality predicts both), in which case multi-task learning does not introduce new information but instead provides a redundant path to the same outcome, inflating apparent multi-task benefit.
all_issues_adjudicated: The treatment efficacy label is derived from 14-day post-discharge assessments whose availability is correlated with readmission risk — missing efficacy labels are not random, and retaining these patients creates a corrupted auxiliary training signal that asymmetrically affects the multi-task model.
Section 7 explicitly states the test set is used to verify model selection and guide the decision to retrain on train+validation — this is direct test set contamination for training decisions.

Preprocessing transformers are fit on the combined train+validation set — using validation statistics in preprocessing means the validation set cannot serve as a truly independent model selection criterion.

The 'efficiency' hypothesis requires combining AUROC and model complexity into a single comparable quantity, but no formal Pareto analysis or tradeoff weighting function is specified — the claim is not operationally testable as stated.

The efficacy auxiliary outcome may be a proxy for readmission risk rather than an independent signal (discharge quality predicts both), in which case multi-task learning does not introduce new information but instead provides a redundant path to the same outcome, inflating apparent multi-task benefit.
